sop.coo_evaluation:
# Fractional COO Assessment — The Forge

**Gaps — no SOP yet**

- **Member complaints & incident escalation** — no documented pathway for handling injury reports, billing disputes, harassment, or safeguarding concerns; creates legal/duty-of-care risk
- **Retention win-back campaigns** — no SOP for re-engaging lapsed members or churned cohorts; marketing has 10/12 but this isn't a campaign workflow
- **Financial reconciliation & month-end close** — no documented process for revenue verification, refund handling, or cash/card discrepancies
- **New coach onboarding (full cycle)** — you have 17/17 coach SOPs but likely no single document covering hiring → induction → first class → 90-day check-in
- **Health & Safety incident response** — no documented protocol for accidents, near-misses, or equipment failure; separate from complaints
- **Member data privacy & GDPR requests** — no SOP for handling data subject access requests or deletion; GDPR applies even to small gyms
- **Capacity management & class waitlists** — no process for managing sold-out classes, cancellations, or member communication during peaks
- **Supplier & vendor management** — no documented approval, ordering, or dispute resolution process

**Needs updating**

- **Email Newsletter & Blog Post SOPs** — you have 2/12 marketing gaps here; likely missing: submission deadlines, approval sign-off, content calendar link, frequency lock-in, performance reporting cadence
- **Client onboarding** — 22/22 is impressive but verify it covers: first contact → trial → conversion conversation → payment setup → first session → 30-day check-in as a *single narrative*; bet money it's fragmented across docs
- **Class cancellation protocol** — under Coaches/Daily/Weekly, almost certainly

kpi.advice:
# THE FORGE — FRACTIONAL CFO BRIEF

## REVENUE — BIGGEST PRIORITY

**June vs May: Revenue down £2,488 (–12.3%), despite 25 more active members (147 vs 122). AR/M cratered to £120.48 from £165.56 — pricing is leaking badly.**

- **Pricing audit now.** You've doubled studio capacity post-April; members are not paying proportionally. Conduct immediate cohort analysis — are new members signing up at a lower tier? Are you discounting introductory rates beyond ROI? Raise entry price and intro packages by 10–15% within 30 days; grandfather existing members at current rate to protect retention. Target AR/M back to ≥£155/member minimum.
- **Churn spike in April–May (8.33% and 5.74%) is cooling now (0% in June) but still erratic.** Implement automated email re-engagement for any member absent >2 weeks; offer one free class or accessory (band, water bottle) rather than discounts. Retention saves 5× the cost of acquisition.
- **Upsell nutrition/PT packages.** With 147 members in a larger space, you can support 1 PT per shift. PT add-ons at £25/session would yield £500–800/month on 20–30% uptake. Offer taster sessions to June cohort immediately.

---

## EXPENSES — URGENT CONTROL NEEDED

**May–June trend: Non-people costs remain elevated (£9,233 in May, £9,390 in June) despite no April fit-out. People costs stable at £3,550–£3,686. The new studio's ongoing run rate is overheating margins.**

- **Audit non-people spend item-by-item.** Utilities, rent, insurance, supplies — identify the three largest line items (likely rent and utilities for a doubled space). Negotiate a 3-month energy audit with supplier; renegotiate insurance with 2–3 quotes; review equipment maintenance contracts — are you maintaining twice as much kit? £9,300/month non-people cost is 47% of June revenue. Target ≤40% (≤£7,100) within 90 days.
- **Staffing efficiency check.** £3,686 in June across how many FTE? Class timetable is likely underutilized still — avoid adding staff hours until AR/M recovers and occupancy per class averages >60%. If you're running 20+ classes/week, consolidate low-attendance slots and shift staff to peak

staff.advice:
• **Redistribute Grace's classes immediately**: Her Build: Lower (17), Build: Upper (9), Elevate (6), and Strongevity (3) must move to JoJo and Eilis. Start with Eilis taking Elevate and Strongevity (lighter coaching demand, builds her confidence); JoJo absorbs Build: Lower and the remaining Build: Upper.

• **JoJo is significantly over-loaded at 133 sessions**: Even after Grace's classes are redistributed, she'll remain the workhorse. Cap her at ~95–100 sessions/month by deliberately moving some PT clients to Eilis (target: Eilis grows to 15–18 PT slots, JoJo drops to 25–30).

• **Assign Body Composition scans to a rotating rota**: Grace's 9 and JoJo's 20 scans are administratively heavy. Create a simple duty list so both JoJo and Eilis share scan slots equally—this frees JoJo's calendar for teaching and genuine PT work.

• **No Sweat Intros (Grace's 3) should be onboarded onto JoJo or a structured group induction slot**: Don't leave these ad-hoc; bundle them into a monthly group session or hand to JoJo with a script to keep them brief.

• **Eilis is ready to step up to 60–70 sessions/month**: She has the PT foundation and class experience; investing her in Build classes and mid-tier PT clients now builds resilience and prevents total dependence on JoJo for continuity.

class_stats.suggestion:
**For quiet studio time gaps:**

1. Launch a "Build Basics" workshop series (30 mins, £8-12 per person) on Thursday evenings targeting newer members who attend Build classes—teach form, progressions, and confidence-building between your existing scheduled classes to convert casual attendees into regular bookers.

2. Open a Saturday morning open-gym block (7-9am, £5 drop-in or membership add-on) since Saturday is your quietest day—pair it with a staff member available for form checks to capture members who can't make weekday classes.

**For quieter classes themselves:**

3. Move **Engine** and **Capacity** to Wednesday or Friday (your busiest days) at off-peak times—they're conditioning/cardio classes that might attract Build class attendees as complementary training, and the busier gym atmosphere often drives signups for smaller group formats.

4. Rebrand **Barbelle** and **Strongevity** with 2-week intro offers (first session free or £3) and send targeted invites to your Build class regulars—these niche classes likely underperform due to unclear positioning rather than lack of demand.

kpis.Action: INTERNAL: Create clear service menu (for coaches and for members) — what we offer, what it costs, what's included.
kpis.advice:
# Service Menu Execution for The Forge

• **Audit what you're ACTUALLY selling right now** — Get your coaches to list every service/package they've sold in the past 3 months (memberships, PT sessions, challenges, nutrition plans, etc.). You'll likely find you're already offering things not on any official menu. Codify the real revenue streams first, then price them properly.

• **Separate the two menus by decision-maker** — Coaches need: service descriptions, upsell sequencing, and the actual margin/commission they earn on each offering (motivates them to sell the right things). Members need: pricing, what's included per tier, and the outcome they'll get—zero jargon.

• **Price anchoring move** — Set your base membership at a "good" tier (not cheapest), then create a premium tier 30-40% higher with add-ons (dedicated coach, nutrition tracking, priority booking). This makes standard feel like smart value. Most gyms underprice their core offering.

• **Make it a one-page visual, not a brochure** — Coaches won't memorize a 10-page document. Create a laminated wall chart in the gym AND a simple single-page PDF for new members. If it doesn't fit on one page, you're offering too much or explaining poorly.

• **Test it internally first** — Have 3-4 coaches pitch your new menu to each other for feedback. They'll spot confusion or missing upsells before a potential member does. Then track which packages sell in week one—data tells you if positioning is working.

kpis.Action: EXTERNAL: Refine our funnel landing pages to make it very clear what we offer, look at common questions
kpis.advice:
# Landing Page Refinement – Concrete Actions for Grace

• **Audit your actual drop-off points first** – Pull analytics from each landing page (sign-ups, trial bookings, contact form completions). Where do visitors leave? That's your real problem, not a guess. Fix the broken step before redesigning.

• **Mine your intake conversations and emails** – Have your front desk/sales person log the 5-10 questions prospects ask most. Add FAQ sections directly above your CTA buttons on those pages. "Do you have childcare?" "What if I'm completely unfit?" – answer these *before* people have to ask.

• **Test one clarity change at a time** – Pick your highest-traffic landing page. Change ONE headline to be brutally specific (e.g., "Women-Only Strength Training for Busy Belfast Professionals" vs. generic "Fitness Classes"). Run it for 2 weeks. Measure conversion lift. Then test the next element.

• **Make your offer absurdly obvious** – If someone lands on your page in 3 seconds, they should know: (1) What The Forge is, (2) Who it's for, (3) What the first step is (free trial? consultation?). Remove every word that doesn't do that job.

• **Create a "from prospect perspective" checklist** – Walk through each page as a cold visitor. Where do YOU get confused about pricing, class times, location, or what happens next? Those are gaps to fix immediately.

What's your current highest-traffic landing page? Start there.

kpis.Action: Implement internal member feedback system (simple monthly form or post-class check-in)
kpis.advice:
# Execute Member Feedback System — The Forge

**Get it live in 2 weeks, not 2 months:**

• **Start stupidly simple — QR code + Google Form.** Post it on the gym door, by the mirrors, on class exit. One question: "What would make your next visit better?" That's it. Don't overthink. More questions = lower completion rates. You can layer complexity later.

• **Embed it into existing touchpoints, don't add another.** Have your front desk ask one verbal question during check-in ("Any feedback this week?") and log it in a notebook. Pair it with the QR form for people who prefer writing. This feels organic, not like homework.

• **Set a 10-minute monthly review ritual — same day, same time.** First Monday, 9 AM. You read responses alone, identify the top 3 themes, pick ONE to action next month. Share the win with your team at their next briefing ("Members asked for post-6pm classes, we're piloting two this month"). This closes the loop so people feel heard.

• **Route feedback differently based on type.** Complaint about temperature? Facilities issue — fix it. Request for a specific class format? Test it with 5 interested members before rolling out. Compliment? Screenshot it, use it in your marketing. Don't treat all feedback equally.

**Real win:** You'll have actionable intel in 30 days for near-zero extra operational load.

kpis.Action: Write SOP's for membership members, personal training members, fuel forward, well75, strongevity. Every service should have a clear SOP.
kpis.advice:
# SOP Execution Plan for The Forge

**1. Audit what already exists (2 hours max)**
Before writing, map out: which services have *any* documented process (even scattered notes/emails)? Which are pure tribal knowledge? This tells you what's truly missing vs. what needs tidying. Don't start from zero where you don't have to.

**2. Use your best practitioners as the source, not your head**
Your most consistent personal trainer, your best fuel forward advisor—have them walk through *exactly* what they do with a new client step-by-step. Record audio or video. This beats guessing and catches the nuances that actually drive retention. Takes 1 hour per service max.

**3. Structure all 5 SOPs identically (crucial for staff adoption)**
Same template for each: Welcome/onboarding → Session flow → Problem-solving → Upsell/retention moment → Handoff/follow-up. Staff will actually *use* docs that are predictable. Variations confuse people.

**4. Make them action-focused, not policy-heavy**
Write "First session: Take membership photo, run 5-min fitness chat, assign 3 starter goals" not "Establish client baseline understanding." Your team reads the first version; they ignore the second.

**5. Deploy in phases, not all at once**
Week 1: Personal training SOP (highest revenue impact). Week 2-3: Membership + Fuel Forward. Week 4: Well75 + Strongevity. Test each with 2-3 staff first. Refine before rolling out. Prevents overwhelm and catches gaps.

**Timeline: 3 weeks, 4-

kpis.Action: Establish a consistent social media content calendar with 3–4 posts/week planned in advance
kpis.advice:
# Social Media Content Calendar Execution for The Forge

• **Batch-create content NOW in 2-hour blocks** — Block 90 minutes this week to film 12-16 short clips at the gym (member transformations, 30-sec workout demos, behind-the-scenes). Shoot multiple angles/lighting. This gives you 3-4 weeks of raw content ready to repurpose across platforms.

• **Use a $0 scheduling tool** — Set up Buffer or Later (free tier) today. Load your batched content into a simple template: Monday=transformation/win, Wednesday=quick tip/demo, Friday=community/event, Sunday=motivational. Takes 10 mins per week once set up.

• **Anchor to real gym moments** — Don't invent content. Use your actual schedule: tie posts to class times, new member intakes, member milestones, or seasonal promotions. This makes posting feel automatic, not forced, and keeps it authentic (women respond to real community, not polished corporate).

• **Assign one person 90 mins/week max** — Either you or delegate to one staff member with clear guidelines (tone, hashtags, call-to-action format). Rotating responsibility kills consistency. They schedule Thursday evening for the whole following week.

• **Track the one metric that matters** — Monitor which post types get clicks to your booking page or inquiries (use UTM links). Double down on those. Ignore vanity metrics. Adjust your template after 4 weeks based on what actually drives sign-ups.

**Start today:** Film 3 videos in your next 90 mins. Load them into Buffer by Friday.

kpis.Action: Schedule regular videography sessions every 8 weeks with Jordan
kpis.advice:
# Execute Videography Sessions with Jordan

**Block it NOW, not later**
- Open your calendar right now and book the next 3 sessions (today + 8 weeks + 16 weeks). Make them recurring. Non-negotiable Tuesday mornings, 9am-12pm works best — quiet gym, good natural light, members used to routine.

**Give Jordan a content brief, not vague requests**
- Before each session, send him a one-page shot list: 3-4 specific member transformations you want filmed, 2 class types, 1 facility feature. Example: "Film Sarah's deadlift progression, full HIIT class from multiple angles, new changing room upgrade." This kills back-and-forth email chains.

**Create immediate payoff for both of you**
- Promise Jordan 2-3 polished clips within 5 days (he edits, you post). This isn't a someday project — it's a production pipeline. Tag him, pay him reliably, make him your go-to. He'll deliver harder if he knows you'll use it.

**Assign one person to manage it**
- Don't let this fall on you. Pick a staff member or manager to coordinate scheduling, prepare the gym before shoots, gather member consent forms. Owner involvement = it slips.

**Repurpose ruthlessly**
- Those 8-week sessions = minimum 20-30 short clips for TikTok/Reels (1-2 per week), 1 longer testimonial video, 1 transformation highlight. Budget 3 hours of Jordan's editing time. Amortizes the cost fast.

kpis.Action: Revisit systemize automations for 6 week trial enquiries
kpis.advice:
# Systemizing Trial Enquiries for The Forge

**1. Map the current chaos first**
Before automating, document exactly what happens NOW when someone enquires about a trial. Where does the enquiry land (Instagram DM, website form, email)? Who responds, when, and what do they say? You'll likely find inconsistency or bottlenecks—that's your automation opportunity. Spend 2 hours this week tracking 3-4 recent enquiries end-to-end.

**2. Build a 6-step automated sequence, not a single response**
Day 0: Instant auto-reply (email/SMS) with trial booking link + "What to expect" video (90 sec, you on camera)
Day 2: Gentle reminder if they haven't booked
Day 5: Post-trial feedback form + limited-time offer (20% off membership)
Day 10: Final "we've reserved a spot" message if they haven't committed
Use Zapier or Make.com to trigger these based on form submission—set it once, runs forever.

**3. Reduce human touchpoints to decision gates only**
Your team shouldn't send templated messages. Automate those. They should only touch enquiries that need human judgment: no-shows, objections, or high-value referrals. This frees capacity for actual retention work.

**4. Test conversion on ONE platform for 2 weeks**
Pick your highest-volume channel (Instagram or website?). Run the automation there only. Track: enquiries → bookings → conversions to paid membership. Measure before scaling to other channels.

kpis.Action: Finalise and document weekly action checklist for each role (JoJo, Eilis, Sarah) with KPIs they own
kpis.advice:
# Weekly Role Checklists + KPIs — Execution Plan

• **Start with output, not input.** Ask JoJo, Eilis, and Sarah each: "What does success look like for you weekly?" Write their answers down verbatim. Build the checklist backwards from their actual KPIs (member retention rate, class bookings filled, revenue targets) rather than guessing what they should track. This takes 30 mins per person, one conversation each.

• **Make it one-page, not a document.** Create a simple table: Role | Mon-Fri Actions (3-4 max per day) | Weekly KPI Target | Actual Result | Owner Signs Off. Print it. Post it where they work. If it doesn't fit one page, you've added noise they'll ignore.

• **Lock in their number.** Each person picks ONE metric they own completely and report on every Friday standup (5 mins). JoJo might own "new member onboards." Eilis owns "class attendance %" Sarah owns "retention rate." Be specific: not "improve sales," but "£X revenue from new member sign-ups."

• **Test it for two weeks.** Run the checklist as-is. Friday 4pm: they report actuals vs. targets. Adjust the actions only if the KPI target was unrealistic — not because it felt hard. People game systems they don't understand; clarity kills that.

• **Attach a tiny consequence.** If Grace reviews these Friday and celebrates the wins publicly, they'll fill them in properly. If she ignores them, they become decorative. Make the review a non-negotiable 10-minute huddle.

kpis.Action: Run team vision session — share the SSO, 90-day plan, and each person's lane clearly
kpis.advice:
# Team Vision Session Execution for Grace

**Before the session (48 hours prior):**
- Send each team member a 1-pager with YOUR personal "why" for the gym + the SSO (Scaled Strategic Objective). Make it emotional + business-clear. This primes their mindset and prevents the session feeling like a surprise audit.

**During (90 mins, structured):**
- **First 20 mins:** You talk—SSO, 90-day priorities, and where revenue/retention gaps are. Be brutally honest about what's broken. Team needs to know the real stakes, not sanitized vision speak.
- **Next 40 mins:** Map each person's specific 90-day wins (not annual goals—too distant). *Not* "improve member experience"—actual wins like "reduce cancellations by 8% through exit interviews" or "deliver 3 class-theme launches." Make it measurable and *their* lane.
- **Final 30 mins:** Ask each person to commit publicly to one thing they'll own, and name one blocker they need you to remove. Document both. This moves it from aspirational to accountable.

**After (same day):**
- Share a simple one-page recap: SSO, each person's lane + their 90-day win, and your blockers-to-remove list. They see themselves in writing. Momentum.

**The real move:** Don't let vague department ownership kill this. Grace needs to name *who owns what outcome*, not just "the team handles retention."

kpis.Action: Audit workload distribution — identify who is over-stretched and rebalance over first 30 days
kpis.advice:
# Audit & Rebalance: 30-Day Execution for Grace

• **Day 1-2: Map actual hours, not roles.** Have each team member log their week (not estimate) — classes taught, admin time, cleaning, member queries, everything. You'll spot the real bottlenecks. Most gym owners guess wrong here.

• **Day 3-5: Find the hidden work.** Look for tasks no one officially owns — member onboarding follow-ups, class scheduling issues, complaint handling. Someone's absorbing these silently. That's your overstretched person.

• **Day 6-15: Test micro-shifts before restructuring.** Don't reorganize everything at once. Move ONE task (e.g., email responses to quieter staff member for 1 week). See if it sticks. This de-risks your rebalance and shows team you're testing, not dictating.

• **Day 16-25: Pilot new schedule.** Implement 2-3 key changes based on what worked. Keep the overstretched person involved in *how* their load gets redistributed — they'll spot what won't work better than you will.

• **Day 26-30: Lock it in with clarity.** Document new responsibilities in writing. Schedule 1:1s with each person to confirm expectations. Grace, this prevents it sliding back in week 5.

**Reality check:** If someone's still drowning after rebalance, you likely need to hire or cut a low-ROI offering.

# Cost Reduction Playbook for The Forge

**Immediate wins (do this week):**
• **Renegotiate supplier contracts NOW** — get 3 competitive quotes for your biggest spend categories (equipment maintenance, cleaning supplies, utilities). Use those quotes as leverage with current vendors; most will match 8-12% just to keep your business. Target: 3-4% saving here.

• **Audit your staffing model** — map actual peak/off-peak hours against your current roster. Shift one FT role to PT, or stagger schedules so you're not paying for lulls. Women's gym members cluster around specific times (early morning, lunch, evening); you're likely overstaffed during 11am-3pm windows.

**Medium-term (next 4 weeks):**
• **Switch to energy-efficient lighting + thermostat automation** — LED retrofit + programmable HVAC in a gym pays back in 6-8 months. Utilities are typically 12-15% of gym overhead; even a 3-4% reduction here hits your target.

• **Reduce marketing spend by consolidating channels** — if you're running ads across 4+ platforms, cut to 2 (probably Instagram + Google Local). Reallocate that budget into a referral scheme (cheaper CAC). Most gyms overspend on digital scatter-gun approaches.

**The math:** Hit 3% from suppliers + 3% from staffing + 3% utilities + 1% marketing waste = **10% achieved**.

Which of these is Grace's biggest pain point right now?

# Monthly Finance KPI Update — Execution Blueprint

• **Automate the data pull first** — Set up a simple Google Sheet that pulls transactions directly from your gym's payment processor (Stripe, Square, etc.) and accounting software. Grace updates once, then it runs itself. This kills the manual grind that makes KPI reviews feel like a chore.

• **Track the 4 metrics that actually matter for a gym** — Monthly Recurring Revenue (MRR), churn rate, average revenue per member, and cost per acquisition. Everything else is noise. Plot these on a single dashboard so Grace can spot trends in 60 seconds, not buried in spreadsheets.

• **Schedule 15 minutes on the last business day of each month** — Block it, non-negotiable. Do it before you do anything else. Compare this month vs. last month vs. same month last year. Write down one insight ("churn spiked — check if billing issues?" or "new referral channel worked"). This discipline compounds.

• **Link KPIs directly to your growth actions** — If you're running a membership drive, watch whether CAC improved. If you added classes, track if MRR increased. Make the connection explicit so you know what's actually working vs. what's burning cash.

• **Flag red flags in writing** — If churn creeps up or MRR dips, write it down immediately. Don't wait for a quarterly review. Early pattern spotting = faster fixes.

What specific KPI is Grace worried about right now?

# Financial Forecasting Execution for The Forge

• **Grab last 12 months of bank statements today.** Open a spreadsheet, plot monthly revenue, member churn, and class attendance side-by-side. This shows you the actual patterns (seasonality around New Year, summer holidays, etc.) that generic forecasts miss. You need this baseline before projecting anything.

• **Build a three-scenario model (pessimistic/base/optimistic).** For base case: grow membership 5% monthly for next 6 months, hold prices flat. Pessimistic: 2% growth, one trainer leaves. Optimistic: launch 2 new class times, hit 10% growth. Map each to monthly cash outflows (payroll, rent, inventory). This shows you where cash crunches actually happen.

• **Front-load member acquisition costs.** You likely underestimate how much it costs to acquire a new member in Belfast's market. Calculate: cost-per-acquisition (ad spend ÷ new members) for each channel (social, local partnerships, word-of-mouth). Plug realistic numbers into your forecast, not wishes.

• **Set a 13-week rolling forecast (not annual).** Reforecast every Friday using actual data from the week. This catches cash problems 2-3 weeks early instead of being blindsided. Annual forecasts are lies—weekly ones keep you agile.

• **Use this to price your next membership tier.** Once you know unit economics, you can justify raising prices or adding premium offerings (personal training packages, nutrition plans). Forecasting isn't just about survival—it's your pricing lever.

What's your biggest revenue source right now—memberships, class passes

kpis.Action: Map profitability of each service (classes vs PT vs add-ons) — build a simple per-service P&L
kpis.advice:
# Profitability Mapping for The Forge

**Here's exactly how Grace builds this in 2-3 hours:**

• **Start with revenue first (the easy part):** Pull last 3 months of transaction data from your gym management system. Tag each sale as Class, PT Session, or Add-on (merchandise, supplements, guest passes). Calculate: total revenue per category ÷ number of units sold = average revenue per transaction. This is your baseline.

• **Attack costs ruthlessly — don't estimate:** For **Classes**: grab instructor payroll (hourly rate × class hours), facility overhead allocated by class room hours (heat/light/rent split by space usage), music licensing. For **PT**: trainer commission/salary + any equipment maintenance. For **Add-ons**: cost of goods sold (what you actually paid for stock), packaging, payment processing fees (usually 2-3%).

• **Skip the fancy spreadsheet — use a one-pager:** 3 columns (Service | Total Revenue Last 90 Days | Total Costs). One row per service. Subtract. Done. This reveals which service actually funds the gym. (Warning: your highest revenue stream might be your thinnest margin.)

• **Identify the quick win immediately:** Look for one add-on or service tier that's >30% margin. That's your cash cow to push harder. Equally, find what's <10% margin — that's your candidate for either raising prices or cutting.

• **Set a monthly refresh rhythm:** Update this same one-pager monthly on the 1st. Takes 20 mins. This becomes your decision-making filter for everything else (new class types, PT packages, pricing changes).

**The real action:** Build it this

kpis.Action: Audit all active systemize automations — remove duplicates, confirm enquiry-to-trial flow works end to end.
kpis.advice:
# Audit Execution Plan for The Forge

• **Run a "trace test" today**: Pick 3 recent enquiry emails, manually follow them through every automation step (email sequences, SMS, booking confirmations, trial scheduling). You'll spot broken links and duplicates faster than reviewing code. Document what actually happens vs. what should happen.

• **Find duplicate culprits specifically**: Check your email platform (Mailchimp/ActiveCampaign/whatever you use) for automations tagged with similar names or created in different periods. Women's gyms often have "welcome new member," "welcome trial," and "onboarding" all running in parallel. Kill the redundant ones ruthlessly—each extra email reduces conversion.

• **Lock in your critical path**: Map the ONE sequence that must work flawlessly: enquiry → auto-response (within 2 hours) → trial booking link → 24-hour reminder → post-trial check-in. Everything else is secondary. Test this path with a fake email address **right now**—don't wait for next week.

• **Assign ownership with deadlines**: If you're using a coach/admin to manage this, give them 48 hours to audit, 24 hours to propose cuts, then you approve/execute in under 2 hours. Dragging this out kills momentum and wastes trial conversions.

• **Measure the win**: Track how many enquiries hit trial booking in the next 2 weeks post-cleanup. Your baseline is probably 40-50%; aim for 65%+ after removing friction.

kpis.Action: Pick 3 repetitive tasks and build an AI automation
kpis.advice:
# AI Automation for The Forge: 3 High-Impact Tasks

**Task 1: Membership Check-in & Welcome Sequences**
- Use Zapier + ChatGPT to auto-send personalized welcome messages when new members sign up (pulls their name, membership tier, first class date from your booking system)
- Set up automated follow-ups on Day 3, Day 7, Day 30 if they haven't booked a class — this alone will boost retention 15-20%
- **Action today:** Export your last 20 member signups; map exactly what info you want in each message; test with 5 real members

**Task 2: Class Cancellation & Reschedule Handling**
- Build a simple Airtable form + Make.com workflow that captures cancellations and automatically offers 3 alternative time slots + reschedule link
- Cuts your admin time from 10 mins per cancellation to near-zero, and prevents members from ghosting
- **Action today:** Screenshot your current cancellation process; identify the 2-3 bottleneck steps where you're manually texting/emailing

**Task 3: Weekly Class Attendance Reminders + No-Show Recovery**
- Automate reminders 24 hours and 2 hours before booked classes (pulls from your booking system)
- Auto-trigger a "We missed you" message 2 hours after no-show, with a quick 1-click rebooking link
- **Action today:** Get your booking system's API docs; pick your cheapest tool option (Make, Zapier, or direct Typeform integration)

**Critical:** Start with Task 2 — it

kpis.Action: Start Cyber security for forge website
kpis.advice:
# Cybersecurity for The Forge Website — Execution Plan

• **Audit first, spend second** — Before buying anything, do a free security scan (use Sucuri or Qualys) to identify actual vulnerabilities in your current setup. You might already have basic protections; don't pay for redundant layers. Grace, this takes 30 mins and saves you from throwing money at problems you don't have.

• **Lock down member data immediately** — Your biggest liability is member payment/contact info. Implement SSL certificate (usually free with hosting), enforce HTTPS across the site, and ensure your payment processor is PCI-compliant. Test this yourself: visit your site—does it show a padlock icon in the browser?

• **Password & access control reality check** — Who has admin access to your website? Change ALL passwords today, create a shared password manager (Bitwarden is cheap), and restrict backend access to 1-2 trusted people. Weak credentials = easiest breach vector.

• **Get a simple security plugin working** — If you're on WordPress, install Wordfence (free tier handles most threats). Set it to block suspicious login attempts and enable two-factor authentication on your admin account. This blocks 90% of automated attacks.

• **Schedule quarterly updates as a non-negotiable task** — WordPress, plugins, hosting software all need updates. Block 2 hours quarterly to do this; outdated software is how gyms get breached. Put it in your calendar now alongside payroll.

**What's your current hosting provider and website platform?** That determines the fastest path forward.

marketing.advice:
**Insights**

Your CPL has skyrocketed 350% (£6 to £27) whilst spend has collapsed 75% (£435 to £109). This isn't a scaling problem—it's a spend starvation problem. You've dramatically underfunded July and the algorithm is starving. Meta needs volume to learn; at £109 you're likely hitting the same tiny audience repeatedly and exhausting them. Your 75% close rate in July is *excellent* and suggests creative and messaging still work when leads come through, but you're simply not generating volume.

The downward trend in leads month-on-month (69→38→20→4) shows deteriorating audience reach, creative fatigue, or both. April's £6 CPL with 69 leads is your benchmark—that was working. You've since tightened targeting, reduced budget, or let creative stale without refreshment.

Your close rate stayed strong April–June (60%+) then spiked to 75% in July, likely because the 4 leads were warm/qualified by accident rather than algorithmic improvement.

**Actions**

1. **Immediately increase spend back to £350–400/month minimum.** You're undershooting the learning budget. Meta's algorithm needs 50+ conversions per month to optimise properly; you're getting 4. Allocate at least £80/week to lead-gen on your best-performing audience. This single move should drop CPL back toward £15–18 naturally as the algorithm rebalances.

2. **Audit and refresh creative.** Pull your April/May ad sets—what images, copy hooks, and CTAs drove that £6 CPL? You've likely hit creative fatigue (same 2–3 ads running since April). Test 4–5 new angles: transformation testimonials, "women-only" safety angle, time-efficient workouts, community/friendship hooks. Rotate weekly.

3. **Widen targeting, don't tighten it.** If you've narrowed to "gym-goers aged 25–35 in Belfast postcodes," expand to 18–45, add lookalikes built from your April–May converters, and test a broad interest stack (fitness, wellness,
